scenario: A FEEL expression must extract a substring: from phoneNumber = "+359-888-123456", get the area code (characters 2-4 = "359").
question: Which FEEL built-in fits "substring with start position and length"?

options.text: 4] -- this syntax applies list range indexing to a string variable, which is not part of FEEL string operations. 4] would be interpreted as filtering a list s for elements at positions 2 through 4, not as extracting characters from a string. Strings in FEEL are not indexable using bracket range notation. FEEL provides the substring(string, start, length) function for character extraction: for a string starting at position 2 with length 3, use substring(s, 2, 3). The start position is 1-indexed in FEEL, unlike 0-indexed languages.

substring() method-call syntax reflex with no equivalent in FEEL. FEEL does not support dot-method call syntax on string values. All FEEL string operations use function-call syntax: functionName(string, arguments). The FEEL equivalent is substring(s, start, length) where start is 1-indexed. substring(startIndex, endIndex) where endIndex is exclusive becomes FEEL's substring(s, startIndex + 1, endIndex - startIndex) accounting for the 1-based indexing and length-based rather than end-index-based parameter.

slice(s, 2, 4) -- this function name is invented and has no runtime implementation in Camunda 8 in FEEL's built-in function library. There is no slice function in FEEL. The correct FEEL function for extracting a substring is substring(string, start position, length). For example, substring("+359-888-123456", 2, 3) returns the 3 characters starting at position 2 (1-indexed). slice() methods, which use different parameter semantics than FEEL's substring and are not part of the FEEL specification.

substring(s, start, length) -- FEEL's string slicing function. 1-indexed start position and length parameter: substring("+359-888-123456", 2, 3) extracts 3 characters starting at position 2. Unlike most programming languages where string indexing is 0-based, FEEL uses 1-based indexing throughout. The start parameter is the 1-based position of the first character to include in the result. The length parameter specifies how many characters to extract. Negative start positions count from the end of the string, so substring(s, -4, 4) returns the last 4 characters. 123456", 2, 3) returns "359"`.

a.text: Incorrect. substring(string, start, length) is FEEL's substring function. Important details: 1-indexed (FEEL is 1-indexed throughout) and uses length (not end-position). Negative start counts from the end. Variants exist for "substring from start to end of string" (substring(s, start) without length).

b.text: Incorrect. FEEL doesn't use [..] range indexing on strings.

c.text: Incorrect. Method-call dot syntax isn't FEEL.
